content.text: Why Autoscaling GenAI Workloads Is Not Like Autoscaling Web Services

Kubernetes HPA was designed for stateless CPU-bound services where a pod scales in seconds and carries no warm state. GenAI inference servers violate both assumptions. A 70B-parameter model requires 40 GB of GPU memory (bfloat16); loading those weights from a PersistentVolume takes 3–8 minutes per new pod. The KV-cache built up during a session is local to the pod and lost on eviction. And GPU utilization is a far better saturation signal than CPU.
The consequence is that autoscaling policies for GenAI must be tuned very differently:
Scale on token throughput, not CPU. GPU saturation appears at ~75% MFU, long before CPU metrics register.
Scale up aggressively, scale down conservatively. A 10-min scaleDown stabilization window prevents thrashing and protects in-flight completions.
Never scale to zero for real-time inference. Cold-start penalty is 3–8 min — unacceptable for interactive use cases.
Use separate scaling policies for real-time vs. batch workloads. Batch embedding workers can scale to zero between jobs; chat inference cannot.

attrs.alt: Autoscaling architecture for GenAI: scaling signals, HPA controller, replica fleet, scale-down risks, HPA vs KEDA

content.text: The binding resource for LLM inference is GPU memory bandwidth and compute (FLOPs), not CPU cycles. The best primary HPA metric is tokens per second per replica, which maps directly to GPU load. Secondary metrics catch different failure modes:
Compute saturation (useful as alert, not HPA trigger)
Latency-driven scale — downstream of actual cause
CPU is not the bottleneck for GPU inference

Configure the HPA with both tokens/s and queue depth metrics. When either exceeds its target, Kubernetes takes the maximum of the two desired-replica calculations. Queue depth catches burst demand before throughput metrics catch up.

# HPA for real-time LLM inference (tokens/s metric)
apiVersion: autoscaling/v2
kind: HorizontalPodAutoscaler
metadata:
  name: llm-server-hpa
  namespace: genai-prod
spec:
  scaleTargetRef:
    apiVersion: apps/v1
    kind: Deployment
    name: llm-server
  minReplicas: 4      # never go below 4 -- cold-start penalty is 3-8 min
  maxReplicas: 20
  metrics:
    - type: External
      external:
        metric:
          name: genai_tokens_per_s_per_replica
          selector:
            matchLabels:
              model: llama3-70b
        target:
          type: AverageValue
          averageValue: '900'   # 70% of 1.2k tok/s capacity; scale before saturation
    - type: External
      external:
        metric:
          name: genai_pending_requests
          selector:
            matchLabels:
              model: llama3-70b
        target:
          type: AverageValue
          averageValue: '5'    # scale if avg pending queue > 5 reqs per replica
  behavior:
    scaleUp:
      stabilizationWindowSeconds: 30    # react quickly to spikes
      policies:
        - type: Pods
          value: 2              # add at most 2 pods per 60s
          periodSeconds: 60
        - type: Percent
          value: 50             # or 50% of current, whichever is larger
          periodSeconds: 60
      selectPolicy: Max
    scaleDown:
      stabilizationWindowSeconds: 600   # 10-min cooldown -- KV-cache and in-flight reqs
      policies:
        - type: Pods
          value: 1
          periodSeconds: 120
prevents scale-to-zero and provides a buffer that absorbs moderate bursts without triggering the cold-start penalty. Size minReplicas to handle your off-peak P95 traffic with 40% headroom.
for a 70B model rated at 1,200 tok/s per A100: this is 75% of capacity. The HPA adds replicas before saturation, not after latency degrades.
reacts to spikes within two scrape cycles. Short windows are safe because adding GPU pods is expensive but not destructive.
gives 10 minutes for in-flight generations to complete and for the KV-cache to drain naturally before a pod is evicted.
KEDA for Event-Driven and Batch Workloads

KEDA (Kubernetes Event-Driven Autoscaler) extends HPA with over 40 external scalers including Prometheus, SQS, Kafka, and Redis. It is the right tool for batch workloads that are genuinely idle between runs and where scale-to-zero is economically meaningful.

# KEDA ScaledObject for batch embedding workers (can scale to 0)
apiVersion: keda.sh/v1alpha1
kind: ScaledObject
metadata:
  name: embedding-worker-scaler
  namespace: genai-prod
spec:
  scaleTargetRef:
    name: embedding-worker
  minReplicaCount: 0           # scale to zero when queue is empty
  maxReplicaCount: 30
  cooldownPeriod: 300          # wait 5 min before scaling to 0
  pollingInterval: 15
  triggers:
    - type: prometheus
      metadata:
        serverAddress: http://prometheus.monitoring:9090
        metricName: embedding_queue_depth
        query: sum(embedding_queue_pending_total)
        threshold: '50'        # 1 worker per 50 pending embedding tasks
    - type: aws-sqs-queue
      metadata:
        queueURL: https://sqs.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/123456/doc-ingest-queue
        queueLength: '100'
        awsRegion: us-east-1
When to use KEDA vs. HPA:

Use HPA for real-time inference servers where minReplicas must be >= 1 (or >= 4 for cold-start protection). Use KEDA for batch workers, embedding pipelines, and offline eval jobs where idle periods are long and the GPU cost of keeping warm pods exceeds the cost of the cold-start delay.
The 3–8 minute cold-start for large models means that reactive scale-up cannot protect against sudden traffic spikes. Two complementary mitigations:
using a DaemonSet or init container that pulls the container image to every GPU node in the pool. Image pull is separate from weight loading but still takes 30–90 seconds for multi-GB images.

— a separate Deployment of 1–2 pods with model weights already loaded, running at idle. When the HPA fires a scale-up event, the load balancer begins routing to the warm pods immediately, then adds more via HPA as demand sustains. The warm pool is sized dynamically based on current utilization.

# Warm standby pool: keep N-1 pre-loaded pods to absorb burst
# without triggering cold-start weight loading.
import subprocess, time
from prometheus_api_client import PrometheusConnect

prom = PrometheusConnect(url='http://prometheus.internal:9090')

WARM_STANDBY_DEPLOYMENT = 'llm-server-warmpool'
NAMESPACE = 'genai-prod'

def get_current_util_pct(model: str) -> float:
    result = prom.custom_query(
        f'avg(gpu_mfu_percent{{model="{model}"}})'
    )
    return float(result[0]['value'][1]) if result else 0.0

def set_warmpool_replicas(n: int):
    subprocess.run([
        'kubectl', '-n', NAMESPACE,
        'scale', 'deployment', WARM_STANDBY_DEPLOYMENT,
        f'--replicas={n}',
    ], check=True)

def manage_warmpool(model: str):
    util = get_current_util_pct(model)
    if util > 70:
        # High utilization -- keep 2 warm pods ready
        set_warmpool_replicas(2)
    elif util > 50:
        set_warmpool_replicas(1)
    else:
        set_warmpool_replicas(0)  # save GPU cost at low load
    return util

Kubernetes sends SIGTERM to a pod when the HPA decides to remove it. Without a drain handler, any in-progress generation is cut off and the client receives a 5xx error. Because LLM completions can run for 30–120 seconds on long outputs, a grace period and server-side drain endpoint are required:

# Graceful shutdown hook: drain in-flight requests before pod eviction.
# Configured as container lifecycle preStop hook in the Deployment spec.

# deployment.yaml excerpt:
LIFECYCLE_YAML = '''
spec:
  terminationGracePeriodSeconds: 120
  containers:
    - name: llm-server
      lifecycle:
        preStop:
          exec:
            command: ["/bin/sh", "-c",
              "curl -X POST localhost:8080/drain && sleep 90"]
'''

# Server-side /drain handler (FastAPI example)
from fastapi import FastAPI
import asyncio

app = FastAPI()
_draining = False

@app.post('/drain')
async def drain():
    global _draining
    _draining = True
    # Wait for in-flight generations to complete (max 90s)
    for _ in range(90):
        if _active_requests == 0:
            break
        await asyncio.sleep(1)
    return {'drained': True}

@app.middleware('http')
async def reject_if_draining(request, call_next):
    if _draining and request.url.path != '/health':
        from fastapi.responses import JSONResponse
        return JSONResponse({'error': 'pod_draining'}, status_code=503)
    return await call_next(request)
gives the pod 2 minutes to finish in-flight generations after receiving SIGTERM. The

hook calls the drain endpoint and waits. The server's drain middleware rejects new requests with 503 while existing ones complete. The load balancer's health check detects the 503 and stops routing new traffic to the draining pod.

Without node affinity rules, the Kubernetes scheduler may place LLM pods on CPU nodes during scale-up (if GPU nodes are at capacity or slow to provision). GPU pods without a GPU resource request will be scheduled but will fail at runtime when the model attempts to use the CUDA device. Node affinity ensures pods only schedule on the correct GPU node pool, and pod anti-affinity spreads replicas across nodes for redundancy:

# Node affinity: pin LLM pods to GPU node pool
# Prevents GPU pods from landing on CPU nodes during scale-up
spec:
  affinity:
    nodeAffinity:
      requiredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution:
        nodeSelectorTerms:
          - matchExpressions:
              - key: cloud.google.com/gke-accelerator
                operator: In
                values: [nvidia-tesla-a100]
    podAntiAffinity:
      preferredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution:
        - weight: 100
          podAffinityTerm:
            labelSelector:
              matchLabels:
                app: llm-server
            topologyKey: kubernetes.io/hostname  # spread across nodes
  tolerations:
    - key: 'nvidia.com/gpu'
      operator: 'Exists'
      effect: 'NoSchedule'
  resources:
    limits:
      nvidia.com/gpu: '1'
    requests:
      nvidia.com/gpu: '1'
      memory: '48Gi'           # headroom above 40GB model weights
      cpu: '8'
The HPA's own metrics are as important as the scaling signals themselves. Monitor these to detect misconfigured policies before they cause incidents:
vs. kube_horizontalpodautoscaler_status_current_replicas: a persistent gap means the cluster is out of GPU node capacity and cannot fulfill the HPA's desired state.
with condition ScalingLimited: indicates the HPA has hit maxReplicas. Alert on this condition with severity:warning — it signals that your maxReplicas cap needs review.
for the warm standby deployment: if warm pods are evicted by the node autoscaler (to reclaim GPU nodes), scale-up protection is lost. Alert if warmpool replicas drop to 0 during business hours.
— more than 3 scale-up events per hour suggests either minReplicas is too low, or the stabilization window is too short and the HPA is thrashing.

Autoscaling GenAI inference workloads requires GPU-native metrics (tokens/s per replica, pending queue depth) rather than CPU utilization, because GPU saturation is the binding constraint. HPA scaleUp stabilization should be short (30s) to catch bursts, and scaleDown should be long (600s) to protect in-flight generations and KV-cache state. minReplicas must be set high enough to avoid cold-start gaps (4 for 70B models, 2 for 7B models). KEDA supplements HPA for batch workloads that can scale to zero between jobs. Warm standby pools, graceful drain handlers, and GPU node affinity rules are the three additional operational requirements that distinguish GenAI autoscaling from standard Kubernetes autoscaling.
